did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper utilized machine learning models, specifically a winning model from the ISIC 2024 grand challenge and an ablation study involving gradient boosting tree (GBT) models and neural networks, to perform automated triage and detection of cancer-suspicious skin lesions using 3D total-body photography (3D-TBP) and metadata.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from the ISIC 2024 grand challenge, including the analysis of a winning machine learning model, an ablation study to explore clinical plausibility, and diagnostic accuracy metrics (AUC, pAUC) on a private leaderboard dataset of 370,704 lesions. It is not a review or summary of prior literature but a primary study reporting new findings on automated triage.

title: Automated triage of cancer-suspicious skin lesions with 3D total-body photography
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of automated triage for cancer-suspicious skin lesions using 3D total-body photography (3D-TBP) to improve screening efficiency. The authors analyzed results from the ISIC 2024 grand challenge, which utilized a dataset of over 900,000 lesion crops from 3D-TBP images to develop machine learning models for skin cancer detection. The winning model, which incorporated intra-patient contextual features, demonstrated superior diagnostic accuracy compared to a prior published approach, achieving a partial AUC greater than 80% at 95% sensitivity. Evaluation on a private leaderboard set of 370,704 lesions showed that the model could detect skin cancer in over 79% of true-positive patients when triaging the top 15 most concerning lesions per patient. An ablation study further explored the clinical plausibility of the triage system by assessing the impact of various input features, including metadata and lesion characteristics, on model performance. The findings suggest that ML-assisted triage using 3D-TBP can effectively support dermatologists by reducing the number of lesions requiring expert evaluation while maintaining high sensitivity for melanoma detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: Included in the diseased class of the private leaderboard set (342 lesions total), specifically 99 melanomas (55 in-situ). The study also evaluated performance specifically for melanoma classification.
Included in the diseased class of the private leaderboard set, with 190 cases.
Included in the diseased class of the private leaderboard set, with 53 cases.
Listed under 'Benign/indeterminate, biopsied' in the private leaderboard set distribution (357 cases).

Listed under 'Benign/indeterminate, biopsied' in the private leaderboard set distribution (35 cases). The paper notes that risk score distributions were not statistically different between biopsy-proven actinic keratoses and SCCs.
Listed under 'Benign/indeterminate, biopsied' in the private leaderboard set distribution (57 cases).
Listed under 'Benign/indeterminate, biopsied' in the private leaderboard set distribution (125 cases).
The study evaluated discrimination of 'any form of skin cancer (including both melanocytic and nonmelanocytic types)' and noted that the dataset included non-melanocytic lesions that lack pigment.

The majority of lesions in the private leaderboard set (370,362 lesions, 99.9%) were labeled as 'Benign/indeterminate', with the vast majority (369,788) listed as 'NOS' (Not Otherwise Specified) under 'Benign/indeterminate, not biopsied'.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions as malignant (cancer-suspicious) or benign/indeterminate.
all_task_types.details: The paper describes the ISIC 2024 grand challenge focused on 'Skin Cancer Detection'. The primary metric was partial area under the receiver operating characteristic (pAUC) above 80% sensitivity, which is a standard metric for binary classification tasks in medical imaging. The model predicts a risk score/probability that is used to discriminate between malignant and benign classes. The study also evaluates performance for specific malignant classes (Melanoma, BCC, SCC) against benign/indeterminate lesions, indicating a binary diagnostic classification task (cancer vs. non-cancer) rather than multi-class histological classification or regression of continuous physical properties.

datasets_used.name: SLICE-3D
datasets_used.description: A publicly available training dataset compiled from seven medical centers across North America, Europe, and Australia. It consists of lesion tiles cropped from 3D total-body photography (3D-TBP) images using the Vectra WB360 system. The dataset includes basic demographic metadata, WB360-measurements (appearance features like size, color, border irregularity), and diagnostic labels.
classes_used: Non-biopsied lesions (clinically appearing as nevi and lentigines)

use.training: Used as the official training dataset for the ISIC 2024 grand challenge.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2024 Public Leaderboard Subset
datasets_used.description: A partition of the test data used for interim benchmarking during the ISIC 2024 grand challenge. It contains lesion tiles from patients not overlapping with the training set. Diagnosis characteristics and ground truth labels were excluded from the data provided to participants.

use.validation: Used for interim benchmarking and scoring during the competition.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2024 Private Leaderboard Subset
datasets_used.description: The primary evaluation dataset for the study, comprising about 500,000 lesion tiles from more than 1200 patients. It includes data from the same medical centers as the training set (different patients) plus two additional sources. Ground truth labels were available to the organizers but not participants. This subset was used for the final analysis of the winning model's performance.

use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the winning model's diagnostic accuracy, sensitivity, and patient-level triage performance.
use.other: Private leaderboard for final competition scoring.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: partial area under the receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve above 80% sensitivity (pAUC>80% TPR)
area under the full ROC curve (AUC)
Number Needed to Treat (NNT) at 80% sensitivity (NNT80% SE)
Number Needed to Treat (NNT) at 90% sensitivity (NNT90% SE)

statistical_methods_used.method: Partial area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (pAUC)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: Primary metric for leaderboard scoring to evaluate model performance at low false negative rates (specifically above 80% sensitivity).

statistical_methods_used.method: Wilcoxon signed-rank test (implied by p-values for AUC comparisons)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the diagnostic accuracy (AUC) of the winning model against prior published approaches and ablated model variants (e.g., comparing AUC = 0.939 vs. 0.922 with p = 0.016; AUC = 0.957 vs. 0.948 with p = 0.068; AUC = 0.957 vs. 0.949 with p = 0.003).
To assess the correlation between participant scores on the public and private leaderboards (r = 0.988) and to evaluate the association between lesion characteristics and ML-model perceived risk.
To measure the association between continuous metadata features (e.g., color measures, lesion size, border irregularity) and the mean ascending rank ordered risk score.
To summarize the distribution of lesions per patient (median = 243, Q1 = 109.5, Q3 = 491.5) and pAUC scores (interquartile ranges provided for public and private leaderboards).

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) between the compared model variants.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a difference in the AUC between the compared model variants.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The study used a significance level of 0.05 to compare model variants. Significant differences were found when comparing the winning model against a prior published approach (AUC = 0.939 vs. AUC = 0.922, p = 0.016) and when comparing the full model against a variant excluding patient context metadata (AUC = 0.957 vs. AUC = 0.949, p = 0.003). The difference between the full model and a variant excluding WB360 appearance metadata was not statistically significant (AUC = 0.957 vs. AUC = 0.948, p = 0.068).

ISIC 2024 Private Leaderboard Subset.description: Primary evaluation dataset containing 370,704 lesions from 935 patients. Used for final analysis of diagnostic accuracy and patient-level triage performance.
ISIC 2024 Private Leaderboard Subset.notes: The winning model demonstrated superiority over the Marchetti et al. baseline. Patient contextual features had a substantial effect on performance; withholding them significantly lowered discrimination (AUC = 0.956 vs. 0.967, p < 0.001). WB360 appearance metadata was more informative than tiles alone (AUC = 0.939 vs. 0.922, p = 0.016). Excluding tiles was less detrimental than excluding WB360 appearance (AUC = 0.957 vs. 0.948, p = 0.068). Including basic demographics with WB360 and context significantly improved performance over WB360 and context alone (AUC = 0.957 vs. 0.949, p = 0.003). At 80% sensitivity, the model triaged 51.57 lesions per true positive (NNT80% SE = 51.57). For melanoma specifically, the model detected cancer in upwards of 79% of true-positive patients when triaging the top 15 lesions per patient (SEtop-15 = 0.7903).

ISIC 2024 Public Leaderboard Subset.description: Used for interim benchmarking. Scores were highly correlated with private leaderboard scores (Pearson's r = 0.988), but participants tended to overfit, with 95.5% of submissions scoring higher on the public leaderboard than the private one.
ISIC 2024 Public Leaderboard Subset.notes: Scores on public and private leaderboards were highly correlated (Pearson's correlation coefficient = 0.988). However, there was a 'leaderboard shake-up' where winners jumped significantly in rank from public to private standing. This was attributed to overfitting to the public leaderboard and limited diseased class examples causing variability.

architecture_used.main_model: Gradient Boosting Tree (GBT) models (specifically referred to as 'boosting models' in the ablation study context; the winning model is described as utilizing intra-patient context and multi-modal features, but the specific neural network architecture is not named, only that GBTs were used for the ablation variants and the winning submission likely utilized similar or ensemble methods common in Kaggle competitions, though the text explicitly mentions 'boosting models' for the ablation study)
architecture_used.input_size: null (Input consists of JPEG lesion tiles cropped from Vectra WB360 photos, WB360 appearance metadata, basic demographics metadata, and patient contextual information)

input_features: Basic demographics metadata (Patient age, Patient sex, Anatomic location, Hospital/institution)
WB360 appearance metadata (Color contrast, Border irregularity, Lighting modality, Lesion area normalized to mean)
Patient contextual information (Total lesion count, Lesion area normalized to mean of patient, etc.)
architecture_details.model_type: Gradient Boosting Tree (GBT) models were used for the ablation study variants. The winning model incorporated intra-patient contextual features.
architecture_details.implementation: Submitted as Kaggle notebooks executed on the Kaggle platform

training_details.dataset: SLICE-3D (400,000 lesion tiles) for training; ISIC 2024 Private Leaderboard Subset (370,704 lesions) for evaluation

classification_or_output_head.task: Binary classification (Malignancy classification and Melanoma classification)

preprocessing_techniques: Lesion tiles were cropped from Vectra WB360 3D total-body photography images.
Images were standardized with respect to lighting and camera position.

Proprietary Vectra WB360 tooling was used to identify unobstructed lesions and extract WB360-measurements (appearance features like color contrast, border irregularity, lighting modality, and lesion area normalized to mean).

Patient contextual information was engineered by calculating data elements describing a lesion in the context of others on the patient (e.g., total lesion count, lesion area normalized to the mean of a given patient) using the 'patient_id' attribute.
conclusions: The winning model from the ISIC 2024 grand challenge, which utilized intra-patient contextual features alongside tiles, WB360 appearance metadata, and basic demographics, demonstrated superior diagnostic accuracy for skin cancer detection compared to a prior published approach (Marchetti et al.).

Intra-patient contextual information had a substantial effect on model performance; withholding patient-contextual features significantly lowered discrimination (AUC = 0.956 vs. 0.967, p < 0.001) and increased the Number Needed to Treat at 80% sensitivity (NNT80% SE = 72.68 vs. 50.57).

Pre-extracted WB360 'appearance' metadata was more informative than raw lesion tiles alone, with the model variant restricted to WB360 metadata significantly outperforming the tile-only variant (AUC = 0.939 vs. 0.922, p = 0.016).

The inclusion of basic demographic metadata (age, sex, anatomical location, hospital/institution) significantly improved the performance of the model variant that utilized only WB360 appearance metadata and patient context (AUC = 0.957 vs. 0.949, p = 0.003).
The winning model achieved a partial AUC greater than 80% sensitivity (pAUC>80% TPR) of 0.173 and an AUC of 0.968 on the private leaderboard dataset of 370,704 lesions.
When triaging the top 15 most concerning lesions per patient, the model detected skin cancer in over 79% of true-positive patients (SEtop-15 = 0.790).
For melanoma classification specifically, the model achieved an AUC of 0.970 and detected cancer in 79.1% of true-positive patients when triaging the top 15 lesions (SEtop-15 = 0.791).

Participants in the ISIC 2024 competition tended to overfit to the public leaderboard, as 95.5% of official submissions scored higher on the public leaderboard than on the private leaderboard, leading to significant rank changes ('leaderboard shake-up') upon final evaluation.

The study highlights the clinical plausibility of automated triage using 3D total-body photography, suggesting that ML-assisted triage can effectively support dermatologists by reducing the number of lesions requiring expert evaluation while maintaining high sensitivity for melanoma detection.

The ablation study underscores the limitations of purely vision-based models and the necessity of multi-modal data (combining images, metadata, and patient context) for optimal diagnostic performance in automated skin lesion triage.
important_remarks: The winning model relies on proprietary Vectra WB360 appearance metadata and intra-patient contextual features, which limits direct applicability to external datasets or other imaging systems without specific tooling.

Participants in the ISIC 2024 competition exhibited significant overfitting to the public leaderboard, with 95.5% of submissions scoring higher on the public set than the private set, highlighting risks of leaderboard-specific optimization.
The study acknowledges that 3D total-body photography technology is currently less accessible and more expensive than standard clinical imaging, posing a barrier to widespread deployment.
Model performance is dependent on hospital/institution labels included in the metadata; performance in new clinical contexts requires reassessment and potential recalibration.
Processing times for the winning model were substantial (70 seconds on GPU, 390 seconds on CPU per capture), indicating potential efficiency challenges for real-time clinical integration.

The evaluation dataset was heavily skewed towards benign/indeterminate lesions (99.9% of 370,704 lesions), with only 342 malignant cases, which may affect generalizability to populations with higher disease prevalence.
The model cannot be applied to single lesions in isolation; it requires intra-patient context (comparing a lesion to others on the same patient) to function effectively.
The study did not validate the model on data from centers outside the original training and evaluation sources, limiting evidence for external validity across diverse global populations.
